content: <page_header: Annotated Bibliography>
<page_header: 715>
Describes a simple model used at Motorola, called zero-failure testing.
Uses optimal set reduction for cost estimation.
Proposes a suite of measures for object-oriented designs. Demonstrates that some of these measures may be useful for fault detection.
This paper examines the ways in which 33 organizations calculate return on investment from software. It shows great inconsistencies that make it impossible for us to amalgamate the results.

A classic book of essays, based on the author's experience building the OS-360 operating system. Many of his observations involve organizational dynamics, such as adding extra staff to a late project. Originally published in 1975.
<page_header: 716>
<page_header: Annotated Bibliography>
<page_header: Annotated Bibliography>
<page_header: 717>
Describes using combinatorial design to reduce test plan development from one month to less than one week.
Describes a method to integrate and extend the best features of OMT, Booch, CRC, and Objectory. Gives lots of well-explained examples.
Describes use of metrics at Hewlett-Packard in guiding maintenance decisions.
Report on software fault behavior at Unisys.
Describes a project management approach that was very successful in developing Digital's Alpha chip.
A nice survey and history of software metrics. Especially good section comparing cost estimation models.
A nice survey paper about different ways to model processes.
Important paper that suggests that we often model what is easy to model rather than what we need.
<page_header: 718>
<page_header: Annotated Bibliography>
An evaluation of the significant activities on 17 software development projects.
Interesting description of how Microsoft combines some good software engineering practices while preserving some aspects of the hacker mentality.
An entertaining, lucid argument for using measurement to understand and guide software projects. Includes a definition of 'system bang' and a good discussion of the meaning of
<page_header: Annotated Bibliography>
<page_header: 719>
Describes the effects of software process improvement and the SEI's Capability Maturity Model on corporate profit and loss.
An overview of client/server pros and cons, plus information about vendors supporting client/server and open systems architectural products.
Interesting article with suggestions about how to improve project meetings.
A product quality model where all subcharacteristics are defined so that they can be measured and amalgamated into higher-level characteristics.

Describes a method for specifying and verifying a real-time system with PVS. Includes the formal specification of the functional and safety requirements. Demonstrates consistency and some safety properties.
An example cited by Beizer as cleanroom orthodoxy that shuns unit testing.
<page_header: 720>
<page_header: Annotated Bibliography>
The original paper on Fagan inspections.
An updated description of the classical Fagan inspection approach.
A good survey of techniques for assessing software safety.
Describes how Chase Manhattan updated a legacy system and focused on interpersonal problems related to information technology.
Old but interesting survey of 25 data processing projects to look at the split between development and maintenance time.
Description of the applications of systems dynamics since its creation.
<page_header: Annotated Bibliography>
<page_header: 721>
Introduction to a special issue on systematic reuse.
Interesting paper contrasting testing to find faults with testing to improve reliability.

A survey of companies involved in the development and supply of complex operational computer-based systems for the Australian Navy. Survey includes feedback on the quality of the Navy's requirements specifications.
An interesting and useful book that frames architecture in terms of the patterns we can
A classic text in using structured analysis to capture requirements.
Discusses  product  quality  from  five  perspectives: transcendental, user, manufacturing, product, and value-based.
Description of a process model used by the German defense department.
<page_header: 722>
<page_header: Annotated Bibliography>
Lovely book that expands beyond software measurement to explain how Hewlett-Packard is working to improve its software company-wide.
An interesting  and  useful  book  that  describes  the  corporate  measurement  program  at Hewlett-Packard.
Explains how Hewlett-Packard is making inspections a standard practice.
This study of designers on 19 projects identified causes of design breakdown, which are listed in Chapter 5 of this book.
<page_header: Annotated Bibliography>
<page_header: 723>
Describes decisions made about which formal method to use during which stages of a large air traffic control system development.
Presents a technique for testing classes that exploits the hierarchical nature of the inheritance relation and reuses testing information for a parent class.
This  classic  work  describes  Hatley  and  Pirbhai's  real-time  extensions  to  structured analysis.
Excellent book describing the best ways to use C to develop high-integrity and safetycritical systems.

Presents evidence that smaller components contain more faults than larger ones and suggests that this may be a universal principle in software engineering. In particular, he says that there may be a limit to the lowest fault densities we can achieve.
Applies analysis algorithms and tools to TCAS II, the collision-avoidance system in U.S. airspace.
<page_header: 724>
<page_header: Annotated Bibliography>
Interesting study that shows how measurement was used to change maintenance behaviors at a large contractor site.
Summarizes results of case studies and surveys to determine effects of implementing the CMM.
A good, basic text about operations research techniques, including PERT and the critical path method.
Good suggestions  for  writing  crisp  Fortran  code, but  much  of  the  advice  is  languageindependent.
Describes how Hughes improved from capability maturity level 2 to level 3.
<page_header: Annotated Bibliography>
<page_header: 725>
--- (1994b). Guidelines to Software Measurement , Release 1.0.Westerville, Ohio: IFPUG.
Standard for measuring general process quality.
Standard for measuring software process quality.
Standard for measuring software product quality, using six high-level characteristics.
Describes the CASE and reuse environments at Nippon Telephone and Telegraph.

A lovely little  book  that  is  meant  to  be  thought-provoking  and  instructive. Each  brief chapter addresses a facet of requirements analysis, forcing us to question our assumptions. Tom DeMarco calls this 'Michael Jackson's best work ever.'
<page_header: 726>
<page_header: Annotated Bibliography>
A description of the MARVEL process modeling language.
Introduction of object points as size measurement.
<page_header: Annotated Bibliography>
<page_header: 727>
Summary of a modeling exercise that applied 18 process modeling techniques to a common problem.
Good assessment of several cost models and their (lack of) accuracy.
An introduction to a special issue on software quality,this article reviews some of the common software quality models and asks questions about what we really mean by 'software quality.'
Assesses  the  claims  of n -version  programming, showing  that n different  designs  share many of the same kinds of flaws.
<page_header: 728>
<page_header: Annotated Bibliography>

Results from interviews conducted on 19 large software development projects to understand team- and project-level problems at MCC.Describes typical communications breakdowns in large programming projects, the cultural and environmental differences that create barriers to effective intergroup communications, and the boundary-spanning activities that coordinate five crucial topical networks of communication. Suggests more effective project coordination, including the use of tools for computer-supported collaborative software design.
Describes experiences with SPMS, a software process modeling system.
Suggests that too much project management structure stifles designers' creativity.
Technical report that defines a process and its component parts, describes a notation for process modeling, and then works through an extensive example.
Describes an early reuse project at Raytheon.
Interesting comparison of different prediction techniques that shows that none of them worked very well.
Describes the results of a large survey of cost estimation practices. Includes information about  how  often  project  managers  use  cost-estimation  tools  to  assist  in  generating estimates.
<page_header: Annotated Bibliography>
<page_header: 729>
Definitive analysis of a famous software failure that resulted in loss of life.
One of the first surveys to examine the characteristics of maintenance.
Describes reuse results at Hewlett-Packard.A careful, interesting study.

Applies Pfleeger and Bohner (1990) traceability techniques to system at Ericsson Radio Systems.Shows that there are different kinds of objects and relationships that can be traced,and that the exercise of forming the links reveals important information (and often problems).
Report posted on the Web of the conclusions of the inquiry board into the crash of the Ariane-5 flight. Interesting discussion of the software design, testing techniques, and proposed remedies.
<page_header: 730>
<page_header: Annotated Bibliography>
Wonderful compendium of the latest thinking on reliability measurement, modeling, and prediction.
<page_header: Annotated Bibliography>
<page_header: 731>
This paper is the original reference for the cyclomatic number.
One of the first papers to set forth a quality model, this paper presents the factor-criterionmetric approach to measuring software quality.
Describes the minimum amount of space a developer needs to work effectively.
Notes to accompany series of three videotapes on software reliability, available from CSR at bi@csr.city.ac.uk.
<page_header: 732>
<page_header: Annotated Bibliography>
Overview of IBM's cleanroom approach.
Presents results to show that smaller modules have a higher fault density than larger ones.
One of the first texts to look at software testing and reliability using empirical data.
--- (1979). The Art of Software Testing . New York: John Wiley.
Still one of the most valuable books to describe the philosophy of testing.
Discusses the importance of understanding culture in interface design.
Discusses the relationship between management structure and project characteristics on successful projects.
This newsletter describes the derivation of a metrics 'dashboard' that depicts a small number of key measures.The dashboard is to be used by project managers to 'drive' a software
<page_header: Annotated Bibliography>
<page_header: 733>
Compares relative fault discovery effectiveness for several different testing strategies.

Analyzes the cause of the Ariane-5 explosion from the point of view of different life-cycle activities. Concludes that risk management would have been the best approach to discovering the underlying problem early on.
Describes a hierarchical model of maintainability, separated into three dimensions.
Controversial paper that suggests that given the right amount of understanding, a process language can describe a software development process, and then the process can be executed as a program.
<page_header: 734>
<page_header: Annotated Bibliography>
Although not a recent publication, contains information that still applies to maintenance projects.
Discusses the notions of abstraction and information hiding.
Included  in Software  Fundamentals: Collected  Papers  by  David  L. Parnas . Boston: Addison-Wesley. 2001.
Describes the difficulties in ensuring that this type of system can work as required, with acceptable reliability and safety.
<page_header: Annotated Bibliography>
<page_header: 735>
Examines Weyuker's testing axioms and shows that reusing objects is not as easy as it sounds. Some of the objects require extensive retesting.
Interesting article about how most requirements assume a benign universe, but security assumes it is hostile. Explains how to incorporate security in development.
--- (2006). Security in Computing , 4th ed. Upper Saddle River, NJ: Prentice Hall.
Classic text that addresses the key issues in computer security today, including networks, encryption, and how to describe and implement security requirements.
Description of effort estimation model based on counting objects and methods.

Describes reuse lessons learned at Amalgamated Inc., a composite of several organizations that have tried to implement reuse, with mixed success. Shows how important business decisions must be made before reuse questions can be addressed.
Addresses the need to allow technology to move forward as you study its effectiveness.
Looks at how other disciplines, including public policy and environmental policy, do risk management,and suggests ways to improve risk management for software projects.
<page_header: 736>
<page_header: Annotated Bibliography>

Shows  how  most  software  engineering  standards  are  just  guidelines  and  presents  a case study  of  a  British  utility  to  evaluate  the  effectiveness  of  coding  and  maintenance standards.

Case study of the use of formal methods in an air traffic control support system. Describes how formal methods affected product quality but also provides lessons learned about how to carry out such studies.
Shows how different maturity levels imply process visibility and measurement.
Introductory text that describes the mathematics needed to understand computer science.

Describes the use of a statistical technique to determine which metrics are the best predictors of an outcome. Very helpful for paring down a large set of collected metrics to those that provide the most useful information.
<page_header: Annotated Bibliography>
<page_header: 737>
Describes a system that uses implicit invocation in its design.
Discusses technique for estimating confidence in software based on fault history.
Two  volumes  that  give  you  a  thorough  grounding  in  the  major  requirements  analysis techniques. Includes  exercises  and  complete  answers. (Republished  in  1998  as  a  single volume.)

This  book  describes  the  requirements  process  completely, based  on  the  Robertsons' Volere Requirements Process Model. Because the Robertsons continue to update their model, you can view the current version at the Atlantic Systems Guild Web site, http:// www. systemsguild.com.
Classic text on technology transfer. Describes fives types of people who adopt technology, and explains how to provide motivation to each type.
Good synthesis of risk management approaches.
<page_header: 738>
<page_header: Annotated Bibliography>
The first paper introducing Softech's SADT to the research community.
Reports on use of Boehm's anchoring milestones and the Theory W model.
The first publication to mention the waterfall model.
Reports on the results of reengineering over 13,000 lines of COBOL code.
Comprehensive  guide  to  using  OMT, the  object  management  technique  developed  at Rational Corporation.
Interesting study showing that productivity can vary 10 to 1 among programmers.
Classic text for understanding the issues involved in designing programming languages.
Uses a neural net on the COCOMO dataset to perform cost estimation.
<page_header: Annotated Bibliography>
<page_header: 739>
Newspaper's description of the difficulties in building a new software system for the U.S. Internal Revenue Service.
Discusses the organization and impact of teams, including how to handle difficult team
Describes billing problems that resulted from a problem with Nortel software.
<page_header: 740>
<page_header: Annotated Bibliography>
A good article describing how six-sigma design is used in manufacturing.
Describes a formal language for requirements specification.
Describes the use of financial incentives for reuse at GTE Data Services in Tampa, Florida.
<page_header: Annotated Bibliography>
<page_header: 741>
Introduces a language for recording requirements.
Presents a model of capturing the requirements based on measurement and goals.
Available at http://www.esw.inesc.pt/ftp/pub/esw/mood/ecoop99/.
Describes reuse program at Hitachi.
This is the current software development standard covering systems built by or for the U.S. Department of Defense.
<page_header: 742>
<page_header: Annotated Bibliography>
Describes a retrospective technique for evaluating competing testing methods.

Presents a method for analyzing processes involved in designing large-scale, computerbased systems, based on characterizing the design process to recognize the diversity of team  members'  underlying  conceptualizations, to  emphasize  the  transformation  of abstract goals into concrete systems, and to distinguish between those breakdowns in the design process that are a part of the design function and those that are the results of the group process itself (within the design context).
Text of Wasserman's lecture about several key issues in software engineering today.
A follow-on to Wasserman's Stevens Lecture, explaining why software development today is different from 10 or 20 years ago.
Seminal work about the way programmers think and how organizations help or hamper creativity and productivity.
<page_header: Annotated Bibliography>
<page_header: 743>
Excellent  empirical  study  showing  data  from  more  than  6000  inspection  meetings. For example, compares  defect-detection  rates  for  different-size  teams  and  different  types of code.
A good, clear introduction to formal methods, with pointers to many other resources.
Finds a U-shaped fault density curve comparing size and faults in Unisys Ada software.
Describes one of the early cost models, using a matrix of costs combined with project difficulty.
Introduced the notions of coupling and cohesion for assessing design quality.
<page_header: 744 Annotated Bibliography>
Description of the operational specification approach to development.

### Index
<page_header: Annotated Bibliography>
<page_header: 715>
Describes a simple model used at Motorola, called zero-failure testing.
Uses optimal set reduction for cost estimation.
Proposes a suite of measures for object-oriented designs. Demonstrates that some of these measures may be useful for fault detection.
This paper examines the ways in which 33 organizations calculate return on investment from software. It shows great inconsistencies that make it impossible for us to amalgamate the results.

A classic book of essays, based on the author's experience building the OS-360 operating system. Many of his observations involve organizational dynamics, such as adding extra staff to a late project. Originally published in 1975.
<page_header: 716>
<page_header: Annotated Bibliography>
<page_header: Annotated Bibliography>
<page_header: 717>
Describes using combinatorial design to reduce test plan development from one month to less than one week.
Describes a method to integrate and extend the best features of OMT, Booch, CRC, and Objectory. Gives lots of well-explained examples.
Describes use of metrics at Hewlett-Packard in guiding maintenance decisions.
Report on software fault behavior at Unisys.
Describes a project management approach that was very successful in developing Digital's Alpha chip.
A nice survey and history of software metrics. Especially good section comparing cost estimation models.
A nice survey paper about different ways to model processes.
Important paper that suggests that we often model what is easy to model rather than what we need.
<page_header: 718>
<page_header: Annotated Bibliography>
An evaluation of the significant activities on 17 software development projects.
Interesting description of how Microsoft combines some good software engineering practices while preserving some aspects of the hacker mentality.
An entertaining, lucid argument for using measurement to understand and guide software projects. Includes a definition of 'system bang' and a good discussion of the meaning of
<page_header: Annotated Bibliography>
<page_header: 719>
Describes the effects of software process improvement and the SEI's Capability Maturity Model on corporate profit and loss.
An overview of client/server pros and cons, plus information about vendors supporting client/server and open systems architectural products.
Interesting article with suggestions about how to improve project meetings.
A product quality model where all subcharacteristics are defined so that they can be measured and amalgamated into higher-level characteristics.

Describes a method for specifying and verifying a real-time system with PVS. Includes the formal specification of the functional and safety requirements. Demonstrates consistency and some safety properties.
An example cited by Beizer as cleanroom orthodoxy that shuns unit testing.
<page_header: 720>
<page_header: Annotated Bibliography>
The original paper on Fagan inspections.
An updated description of the classical Fagan inspection approach.
A good survey of techniques for assessing software safety.
Describes how Chase Manhattan updated a legacy system and focused on interpersonal problems related to information technology.
Old but interesting survey of 25 data processing projects to look at the split between development and maintenance time.
Description of the applications of systems dynamics since its creation.
<page_header: Annotated Bibliography>
<page_header: 721>
Introduction to a special issue on systematic reuse.
Interesting paper contrasting testing to find faults with testing to improve reliability.

A survey of companies involved in the development and supply of complex operational computer-based systems for the Australian Navy. Survey includes feedback on the quality of the Navy's requirements specifications.
An interesting and useful book that frames architecture in terms of the patterns we can
A classic text in using structured analysis to capture requirements.
Discusses  product  quality  from  five  perspectives: transcendental, user, manufacturing, product, and value-based.
Description of a process model used by the German defense department.
<page_header: 722>
<page_header: Annotated Bibliography>
Lovely book that expands beyond software measurement to explain how Hewlett-Packard is working to improve its software company-wide.
An interesting  and  useful  book  that  describes  the  corporate  measurement  program  at Hewlett-Packard.
Explains how Hewlett-Packard is making inspections a standard practice.
This study of designers on 19 projects identified causes of design breakdown, which are listed in Chapter 5 of this book.
<page_header: Annotated Bibliography>
<page_header: 723>
Describes decisions made about which formal method to use during which stages of a large air traffic control system development.
Presents a technique for testing classes that exploits the hierarchical nature of the inheritance relation and reuses testing information for a parent class.
This  classic  work  describes  Hatley  and  Pirbhai's  real-time  extensions  to  structured analysis.
Excellent book describing the best ways to use C to develop high-integrity and safetycritical systems.

Presents evidence that smaller components contain more faults than larger ones and suggests that this may be a universal principle in software engineering. In particular, he says that there may be a limit to the lowest fault densities we can achieve.
Applies analysis algorithms and tools to TCAS II, the collision-avoidance system in U.S. airspace.
<page_header: 724>
<page_header: Annotated Bibliography>
Interesting study that shows how measurement was used to change maintenance behaviors at a large contractor site.
Summarizes results of case studies and surveys to determine effects of implementing the CMM.
A good, basic text about operations research techniques, including PERT and the critical path method.
Good suggestions  for  writing  crisp  Fortran  code, but  much  of  the  advice  is  languageindependent.
Describes how Hughes improved from capability maturity level 2 to level 3.
<page_header: Annotated Bibliography>
<page_header: 725>
--- (1994b). Guidelines to Software Measurement , Release 1.0.Westerville, Ohio: IFPUG.
Standard for measuring general process quality.
Standard for measuring software process quality.
Standard for measuring software product quality, using six high-level characteristics.
Describes the CASE and reuse environments at Nippon Telephone and Telegraph.

A lovely little  book  that  is  meant  to  be  thought-provoking  and  instructive. Each  brief chapter addresses a facet of requirements analysis, forcing us to question our assumptions. Tom DeMarco calls this 'Michael Jackson's best work ever.'
<page_header: 726>
<page_header: Annotated Bibliography>
A description of the MARVEL process modeling language.
Introduction of object points as size measurement.
<page_header: Annotated Bibliography>
<page_header: 727>
Summary of a modeling exercise that applied 18 process modeling techniques to a common problem.
Good assessment of several cost models and their (lack of) accuracy.
An introduction to a special issue on software quality,this article reviews some of the common software quality models and asks questions about what we really mean by 'software quality.'
Assesses  the  claims  of n -version  programming, showing  that n different  designs  share many of the same kinds of flaws.
<page_header: 728>
<page_header: Annotated Bibliography>

Results from interviews conducted on 19 large software development projects to understand team- and project-level problems at MCC.Describes typical communications breakdowns in large programming projects, the cultural and environmental differences that create barriers to effective intergroup communications, and the boundary-spanning activities that coordinate five crucial topical networks of communication. Suggests more effective project coordination, including the use of tools for computer-supported collaborative software design.
Describes experiences with SPMS, a software process modeling system.
Suggests that too much project management structure stifles designers' creativity.
Technical report that defines a process and its component parts, describes a notation for process modeling, and then works through an extensive example.
Describes an early reuse project at Raytheon.
Interesting comparison of different prediction techniques that shows that none of them worked very well.
Describes the results of a large survey of cost estimation practices. Includes information about  how  often  project  managers  use  cost-estimation  tools  to  assist  in  generating estimates.
<page_header: Annotated Bibliography>
<page_header: 729>
Definitive analysis of a famous software failure that resulted in loss of life.
One of the first surveys to examine the characteristics of maintenance.
Describes reuse results at Hewlett-Packard.A careful, interesting study.

Applies Pfleeger and Bohner (1990) traceability techniques to system at Ericsson Radio Systems.Shows that there are different kinds of objects and relationships that can be traced,and that the exercise of forming the links reveals important information (and often problems).
Report posted on the Web of the conclusions of the inquiry board into the crash of the Ariane-5 flight. Interesting discussion of the software design, testing techniques, and proposed remedies.
<page_header: 730>
<page_header: Annotated Bibliography>
Wonderful compendium of the latest thinking on reliability measurement, modeling, and prediction.
<page_header: Annotated Bibliography>
<page_header: 731>
This paper is the original reference for the cyclomatic number.
One of the first papers to set forth a quality model, this paper presents the factor-criterionmetric approach to measuring software quality.
Describes the minimum amount of space a developer needs to work effectively.
Notes to accompany series of three videotapes on software reliability, available from CSR at bi@csr.city.ac.uk.
<page_header: 732>
<page_header: Annotated Bibliography>
Overview of IBM's cleanroom approach.
Presents results to show that smaller modules have a higher fault density than larger ones.
One of the first texts to look at software testing and reliability using empirical data.
--- (1979). The Art of Software Testing . New York: John Wiley.
Still one of the most valuable books to describe the philosophy of testing.
Discusses the importance of understanding culture in interface design.
Discusses the relationship between management structure and project characteristics on successful projects.
This newsletter describes the derivation of a metrics 'dashboard' that depicts a small number of key measures.The dashboard is to be used by project managers to 'drive' a software
<page_header: Annotated Bibliography>
<page_header: 733>
Compares relative fault discovery effectiveness for several different testing strategies.

Analyzes the cause of the Ariane-5 explosion from the point of view of different life-cycle activities. Concludes that risk management would have been the best approach to discovering the underlying problem early on.
Describes a hierarchical model of maintainability, separated into three dimensions.
Controversial paper that suggests that given the right amount of understanding, a process language can describe a software development process, and then the process can be executed as a program.
<page_header: 734>
<page_header: Annotated Bibliography>
Although not a recent publication, contains information that still applies to maintenance projects.
Discusses the notions of abstraction and information hiding.
Included  in Software  Fundamentals: Collected  Papers  by  David  L. Parnas . Boston: Addison-Wesley. 2001.
Describes the difficulties in ensuring that this type of system can work as required, with acceptable reliability and safety.
<page_header: Annotated Bibliography>
<page_header: 735>
Examines Weyuker's testing axioms and shows that reusing objects is not as easy as it sounds. Some of the objects require extensive retesting.
Interesting article about how most requirements assume a benign universe, but security assumes it is hostile. Explains how to incorporate security in development.
--- (2006). Security in Computing , 4th ed. Upper Saddle River, NJ: Prentice Hall.
Classic text that addresses the key issues in computer security today, including networks, encryption, and how to describe and implement security requirements.
Description of effort estimation model based on counting objects and methods.

Describes reuse lessons learned at Amalgamated Inc., a composite of several organizations that have tried to implement reuse, with mixed success. Shows how important business decisions must be made before reuse questions can be addressed.
Addresses the need to allow technology to move forward as you study its effectiveness.
Looks at how other disciplines, including public policy and environmental policy, do risk management,and suggests ways to improve risk management for software projects.
<page_header: 736>
<page_header: Annotated Bibliography>

Shows  how  most  software  engineering  standards  are  just  guidelines  and  presents  a case study  of  a  British  utility  to  evaluate  the  effectiveness  of  coding  and  maintenance standards.

Case study of the use of formal methods in an air traffic control support system. Describes how formal methods affected product quality but also provides lessons learned about how to carry out such studies.
Shows how different maturity levels imply process visibility and measurement.
Introductory text that describes the mathematics needed to understand computer science.

Describes the use of a statistical technique to determine which metrics are the best predictors of an outcome. Very helpful for paring down a large set of collected metrics to those that provide the most useful information.
<page_header: Annotated Bibliography>
<page_header: 737>
Describes a system that uses implicit invocation in its design.
Discusses technique for estimating confidence in software based on fault history.
Two  volumes  that  give  you  a  thorough  grounding  in  the  major  requirements  analysis techniques. Includes  exercises  and  complete  answers. (Republished  in  1998  as  a  single volume.)

This  book  describes  the  requirements  process  completely, based  on  the  Robertsons' Volere Requirements Process Model. Because the Robertsons continue to update their model, you can view the current version at the Atlantic Systems Guild Web site, http:// www. systemsguild.com.
Classic text on technology transfer. Describes fives types of people who adopt technology, and explains how to provide motivation to each type.
Good synthesis of risk management approaches.
<page_header: 738>
<page_header: Annotated Bibliography>
The first paper introducing Softech's SADT to the research community.
Reports on use of Boehm's anchoring milestones and the Theory W model.
The first publication to mention the waterfall model.
Reports on the results of reengineering over 13,000 lines of COBOL code.
Comprehensive  guide  to  using  OMT, the  object  management  technique  developed  at Rational Corporation.
Interesting study showing that productivity can vary 10 to 1 among programmers.
Classic text for understanding the issues involved in designing programming languages.
Uses a neural net on the COCOMO dataset to perform cost estimation.
<page_header: Annotated Bibliography>
<page_header: 739>
Newspaper's description of the difficulties in building a new software system for the U.S. Internal Revenue Service.
Discusses the organization and impact of teams, including how to handle difficult team
Describes billing problems that resulted from a problem with Nortel software.
<page_header: 740>
<page_header: Annotated Bibliography>
A good article describing how six-sigma design is used in manufacturing.
Describes a formal language for requirements specification.
Describes the use of financial incentives for reuse at GTE Data Services in Tampa, Florida.
<page_header: Annotated Bibliography>
<page_header: 741>
Introduces a language for recording requirements.
Presents a model of capturing the requirements based on measurement and goals.
Available at http://www.esw.inesc.pt/ftp/pub/esw/mood/ecoop99/.
Describes reuse program at Hitachi.
This is the current software development standard covering systems built by or for the U.S. Department of Defense.
<page_header: 742>
<page_header: Annotated Bibliography>
Describes a retrospective technique for evaluating competing testing methods.

Presents a method for analyzing processes involved in designing large-scale, computerbased systems, based on characterizing the design process to recognize the diversity of team  members'  underlying  conceptualizations, to  emphasize  the  transformation  of abstract goals into concrete systems, and to distinguish between those breakdowns in the design process that are a part of the design function and those that are the results of the group process itself (within the design context).
Text of Wasserman's lecture about several key issues in software engineering today.
A follow-on to Wasserman's Stevens Lecture, explaining why software development today is different from 10 or 20 years ago.
Seminal work about the way programmers think and how organizations help or hamper creativity and productivity.
<page_header: Annotated Bibliography>
<page_header: 743>
Excellent  empirical  study  showing  data  from  more  than  6000  inspection  meetings. For example, compares  defect-detection  rates  for  different-size  teams  and  different  types of code.
A good, clear introduction to formal methods, with pointers to many other resources.
Finds a U-shaped fault density curve comparing size and faults in Unisys Ada software.
Describes one of the early cost models, using a matrix of costs combined with project difficulty.
Introduced the notions of coupling and cohesion for assessing design quality.
